message:
feat(vdbe): collation-aware NoConflict probe for ON CONFLICT NOTHING on indexed tables

Third installment in the collation-aware UNIQUE constraint work
for disk-backed tables (see the earlier commits in this batch:
"fix(core): propagate collation through UNIQUE constraint
machinery", "feat(vdbe): collation-aware UNIQUE constraints in
MemDatabase + preformatted Insert record path", and
"feat(vdbe): collation-aware UNIQUE index probe via
find_conflicting_rowid_in_index_collated").  This commit extends
collation-awareness to the remaining hot path: the NoConflict
opcode used by INSERT ... ON CONFLICT [DO] NOTHING when the
target table has a unique index.

Problem: the existing NoConflict implementation decodes the probe
key as an index record, scans the storage cursor for a prefix
match, and bytewise-compares the decoded prefix against each
cursor entry.  That's correct for BINARY collation but silently
fails to detect conflicts under NOCASE / RTRIM / user-defined
collations — meaning `INSERT ... VALUES (...) ON CONFLICT DO
NOTHING` would insert a duplicate under NOCASE even though a
NOCASE-matching row already existed in the index.

crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs  (~96 line delta)

=== NoConflict opcode (~line 8753): look up collation metadata ===

At the top of the opcode handler, compute:

    let root_page = self.cursor_root_pages.get(&cursor_id).copied().unwrap_or_default();
    let index_desc_flags = self.index_desc_flags_for_root(root_page);
    let index_collations = self.index_collations_for_root(root_page);

The two helpers are already used by the IndexInsert collation-
aware path; reuse them here so both opcodes see the same
metadata.

Then compute `uses_collated_probe` by decoding the incoming
probe key blob and checking the per-column collations of the
index against the decoded target_vals_buf:

    - If the decoded record contains any NULL in the first
      prefix-length columns, short-circuit to the "no conflict"
      path (pc = op.p2) immediately.  NULLs are distinct for
      UNIQUE purposes, so there's nothing to probe for.
    - Otherwise, scan the first `target_vals_buf.len()` entries
      of index_collations and set `uses_collated_probe = true`
      if any column has a non-BINARY collation.

Snapshot the collation registry once into
`collated_probe_registry: Option<CollationRegistry>` using the
.then() shortcut so the slow path has a stable, lock-free view
of the registry for the rest of the probe.

=== NoConflict opcode: dispatch on uses_collated_probe ===

Inside the `if let SqliteValue::Blob(ref bytes) = key_val {
if let Some(cursor) = self.storage_cursors.get_mut(&cursor_id) {
... }}` block, replace the old direct call to
`storage_cursor_no_conflict_prefix_match(cursor_id, cursor, bytes)`
with a conditional:

    if uses_collated_probe {
        storage_cursor_no_conflict_prefix_match_collated(
            cursor_id,
            cursor,
            bytes,
            &index_desc_flags,
            &index_collations,
            collated_probe_registry
                .as_ref()
                .expect("collated NoConflict probe requires registry"),
        )?
    } else {
        storage_cursor_no_conflict_prefix_match(cursor_id, cursor, bytes)?
    }

The bytewise fast path is preserved unchanged for the common
BINARY-only case, so workloads that don't use custom collations
see no performance impact.

=== New helper storage_cursor_no_conflict_prefix_match_collated ===

    fn storage_cursor_no_conflict_prefix_match_collated(
        cursor_id: i32,
        cursor: &mut StorageCursor,
        key_bytes: &[u8],
        desc_flags: &[bool],
        collations: &[Option<String>],
        collation_registry: &CollationRegistry,
    ) -> Result<bool>

The slow path:
1. Decode the incoming probe key into cursor.target_vals_buf via
   try_decode_storage_cursor_target_index_record.  Return
   Ok(false) if decoding fails (we can't probe for a conflict
   if we can't even parse the key).
2. Capture prefix_len = cursor.target_vals_buf.len().
3. Seek the storage cursor to its first entry.  If the index is
   empty, return Ok(false).
4. If prefix_len == 0 (no key columns), return Ok(true) —
   trivially conflicts with anything.
5. Walk the cursor first→last, decoding each entry into
   cur_vals_buf and comparing the prefix against target_vals_buf
   via the shared compare_index_prefix_keys helper (which
   understands both desc_flags and per-column collations and
   consults the collation_registry for non-builtin collations).
6. Return Ok(true) on the first Equal match, Ok(false) after
   walking the full cursor without finding one.

This uses the exact same comparator as the IndexInsert and
find_conflicting_rowid_in_index_collated paths, so the three
opcode families (Insert, IndexInsert, NoConflict) all agree on
what "conflicts under this collation" means — no more slow-path
divergence between opcodes.

=== New unit tests ===

Two new tests that exercise the collation-aware probe path:

- test_no_conflict_nocase_matching_entry_reports_conflict:
    Build a table with a NOCASE UNIQUE column, insert 'Foo',
    probe with 'foo', assert the NoConflict opcode sets the PC
    to the conflict-taken label instead of falling through.

- test_no_conflict_nocase_distinct_entry_reports_no_conflict:
    Same setup, insert 'Foo', probe with 'bar', assert the
    NoConflict opcode leaves PC untouched (falls through to the
    insert).

Plus an auxiliary test verifying that a NULL in the probe key
still short-circuits to the "no conflict" branch even when the
column has a NOCASE collation (NULL distinctness rule is
orthogonal to collation).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
